curriculum.title: JavaScript: From Beginner to Intermediate
curriculum.description: Ready to level up? Learn arrays, objects, scope, async code, and more. Build confidence with practical examples and clear explanations. You've got the basics - now let's go deeper!

lessons.title: Array Methods: Map, Filter, Reduce
lessons.body: Array methods are powerful tools to transform lists. map() changes each item, filter() picks items that match a rule, and reduce() combines items into one result. They make code cleaner and more expressive.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of array methods like kitchen tools: map() is a blender (transforms everything), filter() is a strainer (keeps what you want), and reduce() is a mixer (combines into one). Let's cook up some code!

code_example.description: Using map, filter, and reduce with arrow functions

questions.question: What does the map() method return?
options: A new array with transformed items
questions.answer: A new array with transformed items
questions.explanation: map() creates a brand new array by applying a function to each item. The original array stays unchanged!

questions.question: filter() keeps items where the function returns true.
questions.explanation: Exactly! filter() tests each item and only keeps the ones that pass the test (return true).

questions.question: Use filter to get only names longer than 4 letters from ['Ana', 'Bob', 'Christina', 'Dan']
questions.explanation: The filter() method creates a new array with items that pass a test. Check each name's length property against 4.

code_example.description: Filtering array by string length
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing a variable to store the list of names. Type const names = ['Ana', 'Bob', 'Christina', 'Dan'];. On the next line, create another variable to store the filtered result. Type const longNames = names.filter((name) => { return name.length > 4; });. Inside filter(), the word name stands for each item in the array one at a time. Use name.length to count how many letters are in each name. Then write > 4 to keep only the names with more than 4 letters. After that, log the result by writing console.log(longNames); so you can see the new array that contains only the names that match the condition.

lessons.title: Object Destructuring and Spread
lessons.body: Destructuring lets you unpack object properties into variables easily. The spread operator (...) copies or combines objects and arrays. Both make code shorter and clearer.
lessons.avatar_script: Imagine unpacking a gift box: destructuring is like taking out each item and naming it. Spread is like copying all items into a new box. Both save time and keep things organized!

code_example.description: Destructuring objects and using spread operator

questions.question: What does the spread operator (...) do with arrays?
options: Expands array items into individual elements
questions.answer: Expands array items into individual elements
questions.explanation: Spread (...) takes an array and 'spreads' its items out. Perfect for copying or combining arrays!

questions.question: Destructuring can extract multiple properties from an object in one line.
questions.explanation: Yes! Destructuring is a clean way to grab several values at once without repeating the object name.

questions.question: Destructure 'title' and 'author' from a book object
questions.explanation: Destructuring uses curly braces on the left side of the equals sign to extract specific properties into variables.

code_example.description: Object destructuring example
code_example.explanation: Begin by creating or using an object named book. Make sure the object has properties called title and author. On the next line, write const { title, author } = book;. The curly brackets on the left side tell JavaScript to take those two properties out of the object and store them in variables with the same names. After that, write console.log(title); or console.log(`${title} by ${author}`); to confirm that the values were taken out correctly.

lessons.title: Working with JSON Data
lessons.body: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a text format for storing and sharing data. It looks like JavaScript objects but is just text. Use JSON.parse() to turn text into objects, and JSON.stringify() to turn objects into text.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of JSON like a universal translator for data. Websites, apps, and servers all speak JSON. Learning it helps your code talk to the world!

code_example.description: Converting between objects and JSON strings

questions.question: What does JSON.stringify() do?
options: Parses JSON text into an object
Converts a JavaScript object to a JSON string
questions.answer: Converts a JavaScript object to a JSON string
questions.explanation: stringify() turns your object into text format - perfect for saving or sending over the internet!

questions.question: JSON keys must always be wrapped in double quotes.
questions.explanation: Yes! In valid JSON, all keys and string values must use double quotes, not single quotes.

questions.question: Parse this JSON string: '{"score": 95}'
questions.explanation: JSON.parse() converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object so you can access its properties.

code_example.description: Parsing a simple JSON string
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing a variable to store the JSON text exactly as a string. Type const data = '{"score": 95}';. On the next line, create another variable and write const obj = JSON.parse(data);. Inside JSON.parse(), place the variable that contains the JSON string so JavaScript can convert the text into a real object. After that, write console.log(obj.score); to access and display the score value from the new object.

lessons.title: Function Scope vs Block Scope
lessons.body: Scope is where variables can be accessed. 'var' has function scope (visible inside the whole function). 'let' and 'const' have block scope (visible only inside { } brackets). Knowing scope prevents bugs!
lessons.avatar_script: Think of scope like rooms in a house: block scope is like a closet - only things inside can see what's in there. Function scope is like a whole room - everything in the room can see the variables. Let's organize your code house!

code_example.description: Comparing var, let, and const scope behavior

questions.question: Which variables are block-scoped?
questions.explanation: let and const only exist inside the { } block where they're declared. var leaks out to the whole function.

questions.question: Using let/const instead of var helps avoid accidental variable reuse.
questions.explanation: Yes! Block scope keeps variables contained, reducing bugs from unexpected overwrites.

questions.question: Declare a block-scoped variable 'x' inside an if block
questions.explanation: Use let or const inside curly braces to create block-scoped variables that only exist within that block.

code_example.description: Block-scoped variable example
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an if statement such as if (true) {. The condition can be true because the main goal is to create a block. Inside the curly brackets, write let x = 10;. The keyword let makes x block-scoped, which means it exists only inside that pair of curly brackets. You can then write console.log(x); inside the block to show its value. After that, close the curly bracket. Do not try to use x outside the block, because it will no longer exist there.

lessons.title: Closures: Functions That Remember
lessons.body: A closure is a function that 'remembers' variables from where it was created, even after that code has finished running. It's like a function with a backpack full of variables it can use later.
lessons.avatar_script: Imagine a chef who writes a recipe and puts secret ingredients in a backpack. Later, anyone who uses the recipe still has access to those secret ingredients. That's a closure!

code_example.description: Simple closure example with a counter

questions.question: What does a closure allow a function to do?
options: Access variables from its outer scope
questions.answer: Access variables from its outer scope
questions.explanation: Closures 'close over' variables from their creation context, keeping them alive for later use.

questions.question: Closures are commonly used for creating private variables.
questions.explanation: Yes! Because the inner function can access outer variables, but code outside can't - perfect for privacy.

questions.question: Create a closure that remembers a 'name' and returns a greeting
questions.explanation: Return an inner function from an outer function. The inner function can access the outer function's parameters.

code_example.description: Closure that captures and uses a parameter
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an outer function, for example function makeGreeter(name) {. The parameter name will store the value you want the function to remember. Inside that outer function, return another function by writing return function() { return 'Hello, ' + name; };. The inner function uses name even after the outer function has finished running. After that, close the outer function. Then create a variable such as const greetAlex = makeGreeter('Alex'); and call it with console.log(greetAlex()); to see the greeting.

lessons.title: The 'this' Keyword Explained
lessons.body: 'this' refers to the object that is currently running a function. Its value changes based on how the function is called. In methods, 'this' is the object. In regular functions, it can be global or undefined.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of 'this' like a mirror: it shows you who's calling the function. If a dog.bark() runs, 'this' is the dog. If bark() runs alone, 'this' might be the window. Context is everything!

code_example.description: Understanding 'this' in methods vs regular functions

questions.question: In a method call like obj.method(), what is 'this'?
questions.explanation: When you call a function as a method (obj.method()), 'this' refers to the object before the dot.

questions.question: Arrow functions do not have their own 'this' binding.
questions.explanation: Correct! Arrow functions inherit 'this' from their surrounding scope, which can be very useful.

questions.question: Write a method that logs 'this.name' for an object
questions.explanation: Inside an object method, 'this' refers to the object itself. Use this.name to access the object's name property.

code_example.description: Method using 'this' to access object property
code_example.explanation: Begin by creating an object and give it a name property, such as const user = { name: 'Jordan', ... }. Inside that object, add a method, for example showName: function() { console.log(this.name); }. The word this refers to the object that is running the method, so this.name means the name value stored inside that object. After you finish the object, call the method with user.showName();. This will log the name from the object.

lessons.title: Callbacks: Functions That Wait
lessons.body: A callback is a function passed to another function to run later. They're used for async tasks like fetching data or waiting for clicks. The callback runs when the task is done.
lessons.avatar_script: Imagine ordering pizza: you give the shop your number (the callback). When the pizza is ready, they call you back. Callbacks let your code wait for things without freezing!

code_example.description: Using a callback to handle async data

questions.question: When does a callback function run?
options: When the outer function calls it
At the end of the program
questions.answer: When the outer function calls it
questions.explanation: Callbacks are passed around and executed later by the function that received them.

questions.question: setTimeout runs its callback after a delay.
questions.explanation: Yes! setTimeout schedules a function to run after the specified milliseconds.

questions.question: Write a callback that logs 'Done!' after 2 seconds
questions.explanation: Use setTimeout() with a callback function as the first argument and the delay in milliseconds (2000) as the second.

code_example.description: Simple setTimeout with callback
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing setTimeout( and place a callback function inside the first set of brackets. You can write the callback as () => { console.log('Done!'); }. Inside that callback, write console.log('Done!'); so the message appears after the delay. After the callback, add a comma and write 2000 because 2000 milliseconds equals 2 seconds. Then close the final bracket and add a semicolon. Your complete line should be setTimeout(() => { console.log('Done!'); }, 2000);.

lessons.title: Promises: Handling Async with .then()
lessons.body: Promises represent a value that might be available now, later, or never. They have three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected. Use .then() for success and .catch() for errors. Cleaner than nested callbacks!
lessons.avatar_script: Think of a promise like a restaurant buzzer: you get it while waiting (pending). When your table is ready, it lights up (fulfilled). If there's a problem, it vibrates (rejected). Promises help you handle all three!

code_example.description: Creating and consuming a Promise

questions.question: What method handles a fulfilled promise?
questions.explanation: .then() runs when the promise is successfully resolved with a value.

questions.question: A promise can only be resolved or rejected once.
questions.explanation: Yes! Once a promise settles (fulfilled or rejected), its state is fixed forever.

questions.question: Create a promise that resolves with 'Hello'
questions.explanation: Use new Promise() with a function that takes resolve as a parameter. Call resolve() with the value you want to return.

code_example.description: Simple resolving promise
code_example.explanation: Begin by creating a variable to store the promise. Type const greeting = new Promise((resolve) => {. Inside the promise function, write resolve('Hello'); to tell the promise to finish successfully with the value Hello. Then close the function and the Promise constructor. On the next line, write greeting.then((msg) => { console.log(msg); }); so you can see the resolved value in the console when the promise completes.

lessons.title: Async/Await: Async Code That Looks Sync
lessons.body: async/await is syntactic sugar over promises. Mark a function with 'async', then use 'await' to pause until a promise resolves. The code reads top-to-bottom, making async logic much clearer!
lessons.avatar_script: Imagine reading a recipe: async/await lets you write 'wait for water to boil' as a simple line, instead of a complicated callback chain. It's async code that reads like a story!

code_example.description: Using async/await with fetch and error handling

questions.question: What keyword must precede 'await'?
questions.explanation: You can only use 'await' inside a function marked with 'async'.

questions.question: await pauses the function until the promise settles.
questions.explanation: Yes! await makes the function wait for the promise, then continues with the result.

questions.question: Write an async function that awaits a promise and logs the result
questions.explanation: Mark your function with async, then use await before any promise to pause until it resolves and get the value.

code_example.description: Basic async/await pattern
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an async function, such as async function loadData() {. Inside the function, create a variable to hold the resolved value. Type const result = await somePromise;. The word await tells JavaScript to pause until the promise finishes and gives back a value. On the next line, write console.log(result); to display that value. Finally, close the function with a curly bracket. Make sure await is used only inside a function marked with async.

lessons.title: Try/Catch: Graceful Error Handling
lessons.body: Use try/catch to handle errors without crashing your app. Put risky code in 'try'. If an error happens, 'catch' runs with the error object. Add 'finally' for code that always runs.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of try/catch like a safety net: you try a risky move (try), and if you fall, the net catches you (catch). Finally, you always bow to the audience (finally). Safe coding!

code_example.description: Try/catch/finally with custom error throwing

questions.question: What runs if no error occurs in the try block?
questions.explanation: finally always runs after try (and catch if there was an error). It's for cleanup code.

questions.question: You can throw custom errors with 'throw new Error()'.
questions.explanation: Yes! Throwing custom errors helps you signal specific problems in your code.

questions.question: Wrap a risky operation in try/catch and log the error
questions.explanation: Put code that might fail inside a try block. The catch block receives the error object when something goes wrong.

code_example.description: Basic try/catch pattern
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing try and add an opening curly bracket. Inside the try block, place the risky code, such as riskyOperation();. After that, close the try block and write catch (err) {. The variable err will store information about the error if one happens. Inside the catch block, write console.error('Something went wrong:', err.message); to display the error message. Finally, close the catch block with a curly bracket.

lessons.title: Debugging with Console and DevTools
lessons.body: console.log() is just the start! Use console.table() for arrays, console.group() to organize logs, and debugger; to pause code. Browser DevTools let you inspect variables, step through code, and watch the call stack.
lessons.avatar_script: Debugging is like being a detective: console logs are your clues, breakpoints are your magnifying glass, and the call stack is your timeline. Let's solve some code mysteries!

code_example.description: Advanced console methods and debugger statement

questions.question: Which console method displays data in a table format?
questions.explanation: console.table() formats arrays of objects into a readable table - great for debugging data!

questions.question: The 'debugger;' statement pauses code execution when DevTools is open.
questions.explanation: Yes! It's like setting a breakpoint directly in your source code.

questions.question: Log an object using console.table()
questions.explanation: console.table() displays objects and arrays in a formatted table view, making data easier to read in the console.

code_example.code:
const product = { name: 'Laptop', price: 999 };
console.table(product);
code_example.description: Using console.table for object logging
code_example.explanation: Begin by creating or choosing the object you want to inspect, such as const product = { name: 'Laptop', price: 999 };. On the next line, write console.table(product);. The console.table() method displays the object in a table format in the console, which makes it easier to read key-value pairs. End each line with a semicolon.

lessons.title: Project: Fetch and Display API Data
lessons.body: Let's build a real feature: fetch user data from an API, handle errors, and display it on the page. We'll use async/await, try/catch, DOM manipulation, and JSON - all together!
lessons.avatar_script: Time to combine everything! Fetching live data is a core skill for modern web apps. Don't worry - we'll build it step by step. You're ready for this!

code_example.description: Complete async function fetching and displaying API data

questions.question: What does response.ok check?
options: If the user is logged in
questions.explanation: response.ok is true for successful HTTP responses (2xx status codes).

questions.question: You should always handle errors when fetching data.
questions.explanation: Yes! Network requests can fail for many reasons. Good error handling creates a better user experience.

questions.question: Write a try/catch block that fetches from '/api/data' and logs the result
questions.explanation: Combine async/await with try/catch: await the fetch, then await response.json(), and handle errors in catch.

code_example.description: Async fetch with error handling
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an async function, such as async function getData() {. Inside the function, start a try block. In the try block, write const res = await fetch('/api/data'); to request data from the API. On the next line, write const data = await res.json(); to convert the response into usable JavaScript data. Then write console.log(data); to display the result. After that, close the try block and add a catch (err) block. Inside catch, write console.error('Fetch failed:', err); so any error will be shown clearly. Finally, close the function.
